Welcome to Getting Started, Lessons with the Masters. In this powerful one-on-one, Clayton and Tony address one of the most urgent problems facing copywriters today, Getting Started. You'll discover how to tell whether or not a client is worth working with, how to ensure you get everything you need to succeed, secrets for extending the life of your promotions, and making more money in the process. And if you're a marketer or business owner, you'll discover how to recognize good copywriters and empower them to produce their very best work for you. Now, let's listen in. Well, this interview is about getting projects started. And one of the things that a lot of writers struggle with is the early stages of getting a project going, interacting with a client, getting everything that they need from a client. And that leads to a lot of problems, procrastination, lesser quality of the end result, and frustrations, other things. And so I wanted to talk to you a little bit about your process for getting a project, not just getting it going, but doing it properly and optimally. And one of the first things, obviously, I think in the process is even before you necessarily start and assign a writing assignment promotion, how do you decide whether or not you're going to take one on? Yeah, that's a great question. I mean, I tend to look at two or three things. Number one, I want to see a product in a niche that's growing, or that's fairly substantial in size. It takes same amount of time to write a promotion for a prospect universe of 10,000 names as it does for a prospect universe of 100 million names. And so I want to see a niche of highly activated, committed people that's substantial in size. Secondly, I want to feel that the product delivers tremendous value and that the benefits that the product delivers are very clear and that in at least some ways the product is demonstrably superior to other products that are similar and that are being offered to that niche right now. And part of that, of course, is looking at the quality of the product. Another part of it is looking at the price of the product to make sure that it really does deliver solid value. So those are a couple of things. Another one that I don't think people talk enough about, but once you've been doing this as long as I have, and any established copywriter who's listening to this will be nodding their head, but the chemistry between the copywriter and the client is absolutely essential. And nearly going, if I'm talking to the client prior to accepting the work, and it seems clear to me that the client is going to want to basically hire me to write it, but then be totally in control of the process himself, then I'm not interested. If I don't, if there's no chemistry between us, if I kind of dread the guy's phone calls or Gal's phone calls, or if I talk to him and I get these long, I had a client here not too long ago that I did a first date thing with, and just to see if I wanted to enter into a deeper relationship with the company. And I was talking to the head of the company, and I would say something, and there'd be this long silence on the phone. We weren't getting each other. We were missing each other. And so there are a lot of little signals, a lot of little flags that'll tell you whether or not this relationship is likely to succeed, or if it's doomed to failure, right off the bat. If I ask for information about the market or things that they've done in the past, what they've tested, or in terms of, well, really everything, in terms of topic or theme or price or order mode or whatever, if I ask him those questions, and the client doesn't want to give me the answers, or if I feel like I'm having to pry them out of him, I'm dealing with somebody that probably won't give me the information I need to create a grand slam for him. And I think it was an easy Writers Club. I was talking about a client that I met with a couple of years ago, and I said, you know, for this to work, and this was going to be a long-term partnering relationship. And I said, for this to work, I basically need to have access to all of your data. Oh, absolutely, not a problem. And so I was there in the client's office, and we signed the contract, and we're sitting there having lunch, and a few minutes later, somebody brings in a promotion piece that they had just tested, and it was working really well. I said, oh, who wrote this? And there was this long silence around the table, and the creative director says, we're not at liberty to divulge that information. So the very first piece of information I asked for from that client, they were not at liberty to divulge. That was a doomed relationship from the start. And so those are some of the things I look for. I can see that. And I can actually, one of the things that really makes sense to me about what you're talking about in terms of chemistry is that I think that you have to want the client, you have to like them enough to want them to succeed. If you don't like them or you're not as passionate about their success as they are, then I think, yeah, it just doesn't, you'll be less motivated, you're not going to be able to endure. It's just like, like, you've made the analogy between a marriage or a relationship. When you love somebody, you know, you kind of, you deal so much better with the ups and the downs and the challenges and everything. And you want the best for them. I think it's the same thing with clients in a sense that even though you don't love them like you do with a spouse, you really have to like them and want the very best for them. And if you can't feel that for somebody, then like you're saying it, it's the potential of just the motivation and the ability to tough things out and to do what's necessary is, I think, going to be much more difficult. So I think that's a great point. I think so, Tony. But you know, this is a business relationship. And one of the most dangerous things you can really do is fall in love with a client. When I answered the question initially, remember, my first answer was, I want the universe to be a large, a large, why? Because I'm going to make money. If I'm working on a project and I can see myself, I mean, vividly going to the mailbox 90 days from today or 120 days from today and having a six-figure check in there, royalty check, then I'm going to put up with the biggest pain in the ass client in the world. So long as he's giving me what I need to be successful. Okay, because everything in the capitalist system is all about self-interest, you know, and we can, we can kind of be charitable if we want to. Yes, we want the client to succeed. Why? Because if he succeeds and we make a hell of a lot of money. So, you know, but there comes a time when, and I've done this repeatedly, where I fell in love with a client, then I saw the client doing things that were self-destructive and rather than just saying, well, have a nice life and walking away and going on to another client where I could make much better money, I stuck with a client for a couple of years and lost, you know, millions in income I would have otherwise earned. So, you want to bring value to the client, but, you know, never lose sight of the fact that we're not doing this for practice, we're not doing this for charity, we're not doing this for love, we're doing this so we can make as much money as humanly possible so that we don't have to do this when we're 80 years old. Good point, good point. And I've actually, you know, the, the, I've been taking internet marketing, he talks about how the single biggest problem in making hiring decisions, which, you know, a client is hiring, you know, us as writers, but in a sense we're also hiring the client to and he's liking the other person, a lot of times it's the biggest, because it's a blinder, a lot of times it's for, it can cause you to overlook other things when you, when you like somebody, but it's all good stuff. One of the things you mentioned in there too that I just wanted to touch on a little bit more is the, you know, you mentioned product and price and it's obviously like those things come together, you know, to help form the offer. And I know one of the things that in terms of learning from you, you know, especially in starting projects is that how much you look at the offer in the beginning. And, and so I, I'd like to, you know, to talk a little bit more about that in terms of when you're first talking with a client too, like, are you testing out to see how flexible he is on the offer, you know, if he's willing to add, if the offer seems weak to you or overpriced or not differentiated, are you also kind of testing him or are just evaluating how, how much he's willing to add to or modify it so that it can be successful? Because obviously if he's not able to make a good, you know, he's not willing to make a good offer to the marketplace, then there's no chance of, of it, you know, really succeeding on the level that, you know, you know, that it takes in order to really make a fortune in royalties. Sure. But there's, there's an enormous middle ground there. You're going to run into situations. Now, you think, think about, you know, what's happening here. Okay. Why is the client hiring you? Well, he's hiring you because he, he has no idea how to create a winning direct mail package on his own. Because if he did, he wouldn't need you. Right. Okay. So now your client, is going into this new relationship with two conflicting objectives. Okay. The first objective is to create an out of the park grand slam home run, you know, huge winner. Right. And the second thing that he, he is just programmed to do is to stick with everything he's learned so far or that he thinks he's learned so far. Okay. So another way to say it is, he wants you to do exactly what he's done before that hasn't worked, hasn't worked as well. Right. As, as what he wants, but he wants you to knock it out of the park. And you're going to find yourself in that, that, that horrible middle ground where, you know, on day one, he's looking at your copy and he says, there's nothing new here. This is just the same old, you know, same way we've done it before. And the next day he's going to be saying, well, why are you breaking our model? Why are you violating the control? You know, and so you have to be careful. I mean, I know employees who have been fired for suggesting in anybody that knows the old Phillips under Bob, Bob King's rule, Bob, one of the rare geniuses and one of the guys I've learned more from than anyone else pretty much, one of the two or three guys I put on that list. You know, the, the, the management, the owner of the company had basically chosen early on that he never wanted to do a soft offer. And yet the first thing that every new employee said when they walked in the door is, I want to test a soft offer. And it got so bad that, that whenever some, you know, we would all sit around and I bet you there were office pools and how long it would take a new marketing guy in the company to suggest a soft offer. And it was made very, I mean, the room would erupt into laughter when the person would say something like that. We knew that he was going to get an emphatic. No, we're not even going to test it. We're not bankers. We're marketers. We are not going to finance, you know, sending out free products to people for, you know, for a month or two at a time, hoping that they'll eventually pay for it. That's not our business. And yet every single time someone came in with that, well, any copywriter who saw that happen a half a dozen times would have had to have his head examined to go in and say, hey, let's test a soft offer. And one of, it's a point of pride with me that I never did that. But, you know, and it was real hard because on the one hand I had boardroom making money with hand over fist with soft offers. And then I had Phillips on the other hand to whom, you know, anybody that mentioned a soft offer is prior. So, you know, there are limitations to where you can go with that. And I think early, no, we're really actually talking, you know, before I even get the assignment, I'm going to ask questions like that. I'm going to ask, you know, what is the flexibility in terms of the premiums? You know, I write mostly for the investment in the health market, right? Well, there are some of these guys who say, you know what, every time we do a write a new direct mail package, we have to create five, six, or seven premiums out of whole cloth. And some bean counter got into the mix and said, well, look what that's costing you because, you know, three out of four of your new packages don't become controls and those premiums, everything you pay to create them is just wasted money. You know, if three out of four don't become controls and you have six new premiums in each test panel, that's 18 premiums that we're paying somebody a couple of thousand dollars a piece to write that never get used again. You know, and so the bean counter says, you can't do that anymore. Well, nobody points out to the bean counter who is typically a drooling moron when it comes to direct marketing, what the multiples are when you finally hit one, you know, and so, but they'll have this restriction, no new premiums, you have to use a premium that's off the shelf. Okay, right? I don't want that assignment, you know, I wish them well. I hope they get richer than Midas, but it's not going to be on my copy. I want to have freedom to create premiums that work with the theme that we're going to select for this promotion. And another one, as you point out, is offer. Now, a lot of times, in most cases, if you're working with an established company, they have been testing offer inside and out and repeatedly tested, maybe every quarter or every six months or at least every year to determine that they are at the proper price point for their market in this environment, because that can change, you know. And so, they're doing price testing religiously, you know, if they're any good at all. Okay, as far as testing, length of guarantee, if I don't like the guarantee that they're using now, I'll ask them, could I test? And then, but I'll give them a specific, and I'll give you an example there. I gave it the other day on an EasyWriter's webinar, but I had a client who was really chintzy, and he offered a 60-day guarantee for his product. 100% money back in your first 60 days on board. If you don't like it any time after that, you get a percentage of your subscription price refunded. Right? And we were getting these huge surges of cancellations right at day 59. And so, Marielle and Trivie and I got together, and she was marketing director there, and we put our heads together and said, you know what, I bet you if we move the deadline for the guarantee from 60 days to the end of the year, that we would completely lose that spike in cancellations we get at 60 days. And so, it took some talking, but we talked our, you know, the company owner ended testing it, and we tested it, and sure enough, it eliminated all of those 60-day cancels, and overall, our cancellation rate dropped by an order of magnitude, because we had removed that mental deadline that our prospects had, you know, boy, I better cancel this now to get my money back. By letting them know that they could cancel anytime and get a full refund, you know, more of them just stuck with the product and ended up liking it and didn't cancel. So, you know, if I see a short-term guarantee, if I see a partial money back guarantee, if I see terms on the guarantee like you've got to return the product in its original packaging, you know, and I see stuff like that, that's obviously a lousy guarantee, and I'm going to want to definitely, you know, either, depending on how bad it is, either test it, I have a test panel with a different guarantee or, you know, just write a completely better guarantee into my copy. So, flexibility is key, yeah. Yeah, and I think for, I love the point about premiums, because I think a lot of the client, a lot of the, you know, our readers are going to be people that are writing for the internet, and so I think one of, it's a good idea for them to look at the client's ability, like, let's say that, you know, he's got a website and he's got different, maybe different info products, it could be dating, it could be health, it could be a variety of things, but that he's got ways to generate premiums, you know, like he's got, because I've seen a dramatic difference in people's ability to produce new content, you know, and I think that that's something to look for too, as your potentially entering a relationship is how, especially in terms of an ongoing relationship, it's not just one assignment, but there's potential for long term, it's a client going to be able to continually produce fresh, valuable content, but then you can use as a writer to create reports to create, you know, all kinds of different things that are either going to, you know, help build the list that you're selling to and marketing to, or to, you know, bolster the offer, so I think that that's a great point. Yeah, and you know what, or if he has capacity to create them himself, that's wonderful. The failing now, one of the best assets a copy writer can have is the phone number of an editorial writer who can create these premiums for a reasonable price, so when you're working with a company that has limited infrastructure, limited staff, you can simply say, well, listen, I can take care of getting that premium written for you, it'll cost $2,500 to have it written, and so, you know, end of argument. Yeah, and I think that a lot of those writers, those types of writers can be found on sites like Guru and E-Lines, you know, to do it, things that, you know, reasonable prices, you know, and Craig's list, all that kind of stuff, so good point. Okay, so now you've accepted, you know, you at least believe that there's, if it's not a good offer, that there's flexibility there, it's a good market, all the pieces are in place, you've accepted it, and you know, the research process begins, and I know you talked about, there's been times when you've gotten all your research done for you in packets, and sometimes it's split, but I wanted to just, for you to talk a little bit about your research process with the assignments. Yeah, sure, well, my research really begins before I accept the assignment to some degree, because, you know, when I'm looking at the market niche, and I'm looking at what, you know, how well their competitors are doing, and I'm looking at some of the direct mail pieces, or, you know, online sales pages, or whatever that are successful right now, that are working well in the marketplace, I'm beginning to understand my, the niche a little bit better, and I'm starting to understand the themes that are used, and the kinds of headlines, and the kinds of promises, and now, you know, are their promotions typically, you know, begin with a benefit-oriented lead, or do they begin with a topical lead? What kind of promises are they making, that we can make too, or that we can improve upon? And so, there are two kinds of research really involved in writing this stuff, but long before I ever agreed to do the assignment, you know, when I'm researching the niche, or when I'm researching the product, I'm really picking up a lot of really good information about who my prospects are, how much they're paying, and by the way, I've said this many times, but one of the best resources out there is standard rate and data service, and they used to be so expensive. It's basically a catalog of mailing lists that are rentable, and there are other SRDSs for other media, and they used to be so expensive, it was just ridiculous. I forget how much it was, but it was like $1,400 a year to subscribe, and but now they're online, and it's much, much cheaper, and you can just google standard rate and data service, and then you go in there and you can research the niche that your prospect is, or that your client is in, and if he's a vitamin company, you know, you just look at nutritional supplement sales, and there'll be a listing in there of every one of your client's major competitors, along with how many active customers he has, what percentage are female, what percentage are male, what their average unit sale is, whether they bought by mail or online, or whatever medium they bought through, and I'll give you a lot of really good information about the people you're going to be writing to, what they bought before, the way they like to pay, you know, are they hard off for people, are they soft off for people, are they continuity people, you know, and you can even, by contacting a list broker, you can even get samples of what those competitors are sending out in order to bring in new customers, you know, because they want you to rent the list, so you say, well, I'd like to see a mailing piece just to make sure that this is the right niche for us, and quite often the list broker will be able to get you that sample, so, you know, that, again, that's all part of that process, so by the time you say yes, by the time you, you know, you say I do and kiss at the altar, you know, you're pretty sure that you can take this product up a notch, or up several notches from where they are right now in terms of response and average sale, and so then, and that's essentially the market research, then the next thing that I have to do really is to, and that may be an ongoing process, but, you know, a lot of what you, a lot of this comes really from the little care package that you ask for during the period of time where the client and you are courting, and you're saying, well, you know, before I say yes or no, I'd really like to know more about what you've done in the past. I'd like to see some of your promotion pieces along with some notes on each one telling me how well each one did, how long they mailed. I'd like to know what you've tested, you know, because they're going to send you control, so, you know, what headlines did you test on this, what offers, what prices, and to get it, to get up to speed really on the promotion history of the product you're about to take on, right? And part of that too is to make them do the work and say, because, you know, they're dealing with a list broker, and that list broker is making good money off of them, so, to ask them, well, okay, well, you know, who are your biggest competitors and send me samples of what they're sending out? They're direct mail, they're print ads, they're web pages, whatever, or give me their URLs for the web pages. And so, you know, the client is very likely to be able to produce that material for you fairly quickly, and, you know, they're, I've found anyway that they're like more likely to do it at the time when they're trying to talk you into taking the assignment. So, plus, even if it's more neutral situation where you're the new guy on the block and they're not really, you know, they're not even sure if they want to hire you, you still have the power at that point to ask the question, and if you do, it's going to create a really good impression on the client. Now, when the client comes into this, he's thinking, you know, is this guy really going to exert the effort required to do, to give me a huge winner? And the more you ask for, in terms of that kind of material, the better off you'll be. And the more you'll establish in his mind that you are serious, you're pouring your heart and soul into this. Other things you should ask for during that period of time is I'd like to see some of your testimonials. Well, chances are, if they have testimonials, they're not going to send you some, they're just going to send you the whole file, you know, and so you'll get a sense of what people are saying about the product and the value that customers are finding in the product, right? You'll get comments about, oh, the customer service was great, I got the product in just 36 hours. You know, and there'll be a lot of cues in those testimonials about, you know, value that the company brings to the table. If it's a product, in the case of a health product, for example, testimonials are basically, what? They're your track record, right? If you have an investment newsletter, you want to know what are all of the, what are all the investment recommendations this advisor has made recently and how they panned out. That's his track record. You're going to feature that in some way in the promotion. Well, in terms of a health product, you know, it's the testimonials are the track record, you know, and I did a promotion not too long ago, we're in the testimonial file before he started writing. I saw this guy says, my doctor says I have a new heart, you know, and then he goes on to tell his story. I was scheduled for a heart transplant because he had heart failure, congestive heart failure, and he took the product for six months and the doctor canceled the operation. Well, you know, that became my headline. I didn't have to write the headline the customer wrote it for me. So not only do the testimonials give you insight into what your prospect is thinking and what he's looking for in terms of the product, therefore, which benefits it makes sense to emphasize. It also tells you it also can begin the copywriting process. You know, I mean, that guy wrote my headline. I got paid for it. I got paid for it, but that customer wrote it. So that's great. That's great. And I think I love the point about the process making, you know, doing all these these steps in this groundwork, that process making you more attractive to clients because I think one of the things, especially for beginning writers, is that that happens is that they're coming from a place of need or desperation. And it's understandable. I think obviously we I'm, you know, I've been there and I know what that feels like. But they create the impression that they'll do anything and just give me the assignment. And whereas when you when you don't when you qualify when you're asking for all this materials and you're kind of leaning back and you're saying, Well, I'm I need a lot more to make a decision. I need to hear testimonials. I need to see research, all these different things that you're writing. It creates not just a more professional impression, but less of a less of a needy one more like your in a sense, they're evaluating you, but you're also evaluating them. And I think that it levels the playing field a little bit more and makes the client believe that that you have other options that you you could be doing other things. But, you know, that you are really considering them. And it turns the psychology around, I think, in a really, really good way. I think it does too. And, you know, if you're uncomfortable, you know, if you're not the demanding kind of person, by the way, one of these days remind me and I'll call Kent Kome up and we'll record the the conversation. And I'll hire him to write something for us. Okay. You have never heard a more meticulous demanding in a very nice way demanding meticulous. He's got this mental checklist. And he will get down to the finest points before he'll say yes, he'll go through the product, the market, the offer, the theme, you know, he'll just, you know, for me, it's pretty much automatic, you know, you say, you know, I kind of get it pretty quickly and tend to agree or not fairly fast. But with Kent, oh my goodness. And, you know, if more people did it the way Kent did, I think we'd all be better prepared to do the work. So remind me that they will ambush old Kent. Okay, definitely. I think in the meantime, you know, the course does have a lot of questions and a lot of material like that. So I think that's the benefit too of it, you know, is to use a lot of the materials that we've given them to, you know, ask the client, direct the client and make sure that they're getting everything that they need. So it's great advice. And it's great advice. The next thing, you know, a lot of this, the purpose of all of this groundwork of all this research and discussion with the client, looking at the market is really to come up with something you've talked a lot about before in the total package, but the course has two chapters on theme and, you know, really the big idea. And so I'm going to talk to you a little bit about your process for it because I think that that you can write about, you know, it's one thing to write about it or to hear about it. But I think people really want to get inside your mind as much as possible in terms of, because that definitely makes or breaks your success on a promotion. It's your ability to come up with that. So if you could talk a little bit more about that, be great. Sure. Well, in some cases, it's really simple. I mean, if you're, let's say you're selling a product that could grow hair on a cue ball, okay? It's a hair regrowth product. And you don't have to be a rocket scientist to figure out that the people that you're going to be talking to are going to be people who have hair loss problems. And that the thing that they most deeply desire is to stop losing their hair and start growing some, right? So that's not going to be a problem. You know that they are very highly motivated to do that. And you also know that if the media people have done their job, that you're going to be talking to people who are balding and who have bought information products or, you know, consumable products in the past to regrow hair. And so you, I mean, let me ask you, I mean, you know, in a situation like that, do you lead with the big promise about how to grow hair on a cue ball? Or do you lead with something understanding that these people have bought information and products before, they're still balding and they're still reading your copy, right? So wouldn't it make more sense to lead with a credibility item? You know, I mean, that determines your theme, okay? I saw an infomercial over the weekend actually been watching it pretty much nonstop for months. And anybody that watches infomercials will know the one I'm talking about. But there's a black girl in there. She was Miss Black Nevada and she lost all of her hair. And I mean, the top of her head looked just pathetic in the before picture. And she gets on there and with the doctor and she's being interviewed by this woman, the spokesperson kind of. And she proceeds to tell her story about how she hated herself. She couldn't date, you know, she would wear a wig to make personal appearances, but she felt like a phony. And here she was a beauty queen, you know, and she burst into tears and the tears are literally dripping off her chin as she's telling the story and how she began using this hair growth product. And in 90 days, she had results and within 60 days, she had four inches of hair all over her head. And they had the photographs to back it up, her emotion and telling the story was appropriate. Now, do I believe that this all-natural product that basically consists of vitamins and minerals will grow hair on a cue ball? Absolutely not. I'd have to be insane to believe that. Right. But that woman's experience and the emotion with which she told the story is enough to get me to suspend my disbelief and to make me at least want to try the product. Now, I haven't bought it. I'm going to hang on to at least that much of my dignity, but the fact of the matter is, and I just wrote about this today, the people you're talking to desperately want to believe. And because the benefit that you're offering is a powerful one, it's something that's deeply desired in their life. And so in a situation like that, where they hear a regrowth product, for example, or the product that makes a certain part of a man's body larger, there's no way a sentient human being would believe that for a heartbeat in it, they sell 200 million pills a year. And so in those situations, you know right off the bat that your theme has got to be credibility. You've got to find a way to suspend the disbelief. If you're selling a product in the investment area, it's a completely different approach. The credibility that you've made people a lot of money in the past needs to be in the package, but investors are chronic newshounds because they know that the day's headlines affect the value of their investments. And so they want and need more topical promotions. So your theme in an investment package needs to be something that's going on now, something that your prospect is already thinking about, and therefore he's either worried about it or excited about it or frustrated by it. And so it just makes sense with a promotion where you're going to invest or newshound that you would use a topical lead. And then there are the third category, really, are products that deliver a benefit that isn't often offered to people and that most of your prospects haven't already tried to achieve that benefit or get that benefit in their life by buying someone else's product at some point and wound up disappointed. And for some reason with Springs to Mind are those upside down tomato growing gadgets that you can buy. And I'd never heard of those things. And here you can have your supply of fresh tomatoes hanging in your front porch or in your kitchen or wherever. And the thing just makes these beautiful, fresh tomatoes all year round. Well, people don't wake up in the morning and slap themselves on the forehead and say, gee, I wish I had fresh tomatoes. But when you see a cool product that offers a benefit like that that looks like it'd be fun to play with, you know, and will improve the quality of your diet, your nutrition, the taste of your food and everything, then with that, now you've got something that I would seriously consider leading with the big benefit off the top. So yeah, that's a good I just let the product and the niche tell you talk to you about the best way in and you'll find it. Yeah, it's a good point. I think we were in and John's office I think the other day talking about how the fortune is made selling all these little households, especially on infomers, all these little household gadgets, but essentially cool, you know, gadgets of convenience, they make things easier, they make cooking easier, cleaning easier, or and their benefits, I think people like you said, they don't often immediately think of it. And a lot of times I think in those cases, the theme or what really what the promotion is leading with too is really the demonstration of those, you know, the demonstration is so attention grabbing because it's unique and people haven't thought of it and usually they position it in a certain way that makes it seem really amazing. Yeah, that's the lead, but you're right. In essence, the benefit and in essence, the market is going to tell you, you know, when that benefit is unique enough and that it will be strong. Yep. Absolutely. I was just going to point out that, you know, in Arthur Johnson's infomercials for boardroom, that's what he does. I mean, he just demonstrates one, all these infomercials are for a book that has a whole bunch of home remedies in it. And Arthur just takes you through with these two crazy old ladies that they wrote the book and, you know, they talk about how they don't call us the crazy whatever sisters for nothing, you know, and they're talking to you about doing all of these weird things to deal with headaches and, you know, sniffing onion or, you know, whatever. And they laugh at the fact that, you know, that it seems to work for some people and they talk freely about the fact that it doesn't work for others. But the entire infomercial is nothing more than just demonstration after demonstration after demonstration of ways to use this book that they're selling to live better every day. And so, you know, that's the spring to mind is I was listening to your comment there. Yeah, it works. It works. You know, one of the things getting back, you know, addressing this idea of theme and big idea with any, you know, going back into conjunction with dealing with the client is a lot of times, as you mentioned, there's this dual thing going on, the client is hiring you because he doesn't, he can't do all this at the same time. A lot of clients come into a project with preconceived ideas of which what the theme should be and what the big ideas should be and they want to tell you that or maybe even if they don't, they want, there's certain ideas or things or themes that they feel like are off limits or they don't want to go with. And so how do you kind of navigate that territory with clients? You know, I would never do that in advance. You know, one of the things that happens is that you can suggest something and because the person you're talking to is not a copywriter, they can't envision how it might be written in a way that could really work. You know, so, you know, if you approach them on the front end, well, you know, how about, you know, I'd like to write, I would like to write on this, you know, and they can't envision it and so their knee-jerk reaction might be negative. And so I would, if, and quite often this happens to me all the time, so, you know, it's kind of near and dear to my heart. I, of course, I have a very close relationship with one of my clients and when I make, I'll make a suggestion and he'll quite often just say, oh, no, that'll, you can't tell people that, you know, you can't say that, you know, and of course you can't, you know, it's not what, it's not what you do, it's how you do it in most cases and it's how the copy is ultimately written. And so, you know, it would be better to go ahead and just write, if that's the way that you feel, if you have an absolute passion for a particular lead, write it up, you know, and send your first draft in that way. You may, then at the next step, after you receive critique back from the client or comments back from the client, you may identify problems with it that you can solve or you may, you may come to the conclusion that there's, it's unsolvable, it's really unusable, but give it your best shot rather than trying to explain it to a client, let him actually see it. So, I, going in on the front end, I try to keep the conversation light, I try to keep it, make sure that it does not become a, become a planning conversation. It's rather a brainstorming conversation in which every idea is a good idea and that, you know, after we're through here, I'm going to take my notes and go away for a couple of days and I'm going to decide how I want to, how I want to approach this, you know. And now, in some cases, if the client comes to me and says, well, here's the thing we want you to write about, you know, gold is hot right now, I want you to write a package about how to make, how to get rich in gold, you know. Well, you know, if that sounds reasonable to me, you know, and in that very specific case, I'd want to establish number one, that gold is indeed hot and that number two, that the chances that that trend reversing itself while I'm halfway through writing my copy are, are minimal, but if it's reasonable and if I can get excited about it, then, you know, why not? And, you know, unless I can come up with a theme that I think is, you know, much, much stronger, if the client, and in some cases, the clients may already have other themes covered, you know. They may, the, if the control is about gold, they're probably not going to want you to go there. And so, you know, because they have an agreement with a copywriter that wrote this great control that you're trying to beat, and they have a, they have a relationship with him, he's written a great promotion, the last thing they're going to want to do is to tick him off by fielding a package that's very similar to his and that by accident or design, co-ops some of his sales arguments or whatever. So, there's got to be some back and forth there. But again, if you feel like even for a moment that the client's trying to shove a theme down your throat and you're less than thrilled with it, my advice is walk away. You know, that you need to feel, you need to be convinced. And really, you know, I think for a copywriter, it's more than just intellectual, you know, being convinced of the intellectual validity of the theme or the approach. You need to be excited about it. You know, you need to have an energy, you know, energy is creativity. And excitement creates energy. And so, you know, if you're not excited about it, you're not going to have energy in your approach to it. And you're not going to be creative as a result. Yeah, the copy's going to be flat. I know it's going to happen a lot. It's not just the copy, but as you're writing it, all of those great ideas that come to you as you're fully immersed in the product and the promotion, all those great ideas that come to you that expand the argument or dimensionalize it or add credibility to it, all of those things, you're just never going to have those ideas if you don't have energy for it. So, it's better to walk. It's much better to walk. And I know that's easy to say for me, but, you know, because, you know, money is not an issue with us as it was in the early days of the book. And you may have to take someone when you're first getting started that you, you know, just to keep body and soul together, just to pay the rent for the month. But as soon as you possibly can, you want to get to the point where you're extremely picky and you need to build a reputation for being extremely good at it. And you need to build a reputation for being extremely good at it. And you need to be able to do that. Extremely picky. And you need to build a reputation for being extremely picky about the projects that you accept. Because the reverse is to accept everything comes down the pike and have a very low success rate. And a few years of that, where it gets around in your entire business is limited as a result. So, it makes sense. It makes sense. And do you ever, I imagine, it's circumstantial, but explore multiple themes and even write out multiple variations of one promotion based on, you know, maybe you feel like there's two or, you know, maybe even three sometimes really, really powerful theme or, you know, big ideas that you can, you can lead with. Do you, because I remember in one of the actually, the interviews you did with Gary way back, he talked about a lot of times how he would often have a very proven safe form of a promotion and then he would have a more riskier, more different, you know, less proven, but potentially more of a grand slam type of promotion. And it was an interesting distinction. And I think that I've seen you kind of do both sometimes you select one that you're 100% confident with and then other times you can explore multiple ones. But can you talk a little bit more about that? Sure. Well, I'm doing it right now. I've got a direct mail promotion for an investment newsletter. And the promotion offers three premiums. One is on how to make money in gold and natural resources. One is how to make money in currencies. And one is on how to make money in foreign stock markets. Right. And all of these reports are within the context of the US recession, the dollar crashing and oil and other commodities shooting through the roof. And so the question then becomes, which of those three topics, natural resources, currencies, or foreign stock investing, has the greatest pull with investors now and is most likely to be our best front end theme in terms of grabbing attention and converting that attention to readership. Now, you know, all three events are happening at the same time. You know, China is growing its economy at 10.5% a year, while ours is shrinking. The US dollars on a seven year in the seventh year of a decline, the biggest decline in history, and oil is selling for $120 a barrel and gold is hit over $1,000 an ounce. And wheat and soybeans and corn and everything else are at all time sky-high record prices. And so investors are naturally, and they're seeing these headlines every single day in the news, dollar crashes again against the euro, or wheat prices, you know, food riots in Indonesia, or wheat prices are off the scale or copper or some other commodity. And so they're seeing these things all the time. Now, you know, when an investor sees, and it's the old thing, you know, that they say a recession is when your neighbor loses his job and a depression is when you lose your job. The kind of reverse is true with investment opportunities. You know, a good investment opportunity is one that you're in, and it's going up by some rate, but a great, you know, walking wet dream investment is one that your neighbor is making money with. You know, it's the grass is always greener on the other side kind of thing. And so, you know, anytime you see a headline that says that, you know, somebody's making money in gold, well, you know, your people have this irresistible internal urge to be making money in gold, too, because they feel like they're being left out. So the only question is, which of those three themes is the one that's going to have the greatest pull? And there's really no way to know, you know, and, you know, you can guess, and sometimes you'll get it right. But the cool thing is that most promotions are fairly modular. There's, you know, the front end of a promotion, you know, grabs the prospect's attention, engages him in conversation, and begins presenting, you know, product benefits, or maybe premium benefits, but still starts presenting benefits, right? And then there's a break at which you begin selling the product that you're actually going to ask money for, and you dimensionalize that product, and you trivialize your price, and you present your guarantee, and you add some kind of an urgency element. And so, you know, that's the, let's call that the back half of the copy. And then the front, but the front half is really where you engage him, right? So what I've done is I just wrote a promotion where I just selected at random an order in which I introduced these three premiums, one on commodity investing, one on currency investing, and one on foreign stock market investing, right? And now I can have three different front ends, you know? In front end A, I start with the China factor. Front end B, I start with oil prices, how to make money in oil. And in the third one, you know, how to make a dollar every time the dollar drops one cent in value. So, you know, I have three different front ends. I can develop all of them simultaneously because it's only one package. I've just rejuggled the front end. Now, I can go out with that, those three panels, and the market will tell me which approach, which lead is the one that's going to generate the greatest attention and readership, and by extension response. So, absolutely. And I've done that many, many times. I started doing that actually, I'd say in 1998, 1998, I started doing that with a newsletter called the Y2K Alert because we didn't know whether people were more worried about disruption in food supplies, loss of electricity. You know, we didn't know which of the effects of the expected Y2K crisis people were most concerned about. And so now an idiot would have done a poll. Okay. A drooling moron would have said, let's have a focus group. You know, and he would have ended up with a bunch of answers from people who didn't have money on the line, which of course are worth nothing. But instead, we did three or four front ends, each on a different theme, and the market voted with their money. And those votes you can take to the bank. Yeah, you know, all makes a good sense. And I think, you know, in the investment market, sometimes the problem is that those themes don't last that long. But I think the really cool part about the process you just described, like let's say that you're writing for help on the web or biz off on the web or something like that is that the client has three, even if one promotion starts to fatigue, he could cycle them, you know, and he can get more trouble. I know you've done this in the health market too. So you cycle promotion so that you get longer, the control lasts longer. But I think on the internet, it's very valid because so many of people like on a house, say, you know, on a house file, they've seen, we've even done this internally. On a house file, they've seen a certain headline and lead for promotion. And they've come to be able to disregard it. You know, with a new one, even if it's maybe not quite as good as the original one that tested best, at least, you know, you're going to get more looks more readership and then you can not take a lot of this pickup sales, you wouldn't have gotten otherwise. Well, we see that all the time. I mean, in direct mail and online, if you're going to test A against B, you know, and quite often C and D, you're going to find two or three that are just within a whisker of each other in terms of response. And what that means is you got three controls. Now, you know, a client who, you know, probably shouldn't be allowed to handle his own money, might call up and say, I've tested all of these different ideas. And, you know, what I'm spending all this money on testing and you haven't given me a lift in response, and he'll be upset about that. Well, that person shouldn't be allowed around sharp objects. You know, the fact of the matter is, instead of one control, he has three. And now he can rotate those controls through his prospect universe. And instead of getting attrition, by showing the same copy repeated times to the same people, he now has, you know, promotions that will last him all year without any attrition at all. Yeah, that's great. So, yeah, that's, but believe it or not, you know, oh, one of these days, we have to do one of these things on dumb things clients say. But that's one of them. Okay, one of the dumbest things clients say is, you know, we've done all these tests and you got me the same ROI on each piece. Well, that's wonderful. We just tripled the number of controls you have, which means we just tripled the lifetime of this copy. And all we have to do is bicycle them, you know, each month, and to keep your product and your approach fresh and new in your market, in your prospect universe. It's a wonderful thing. And, you know, in some cases, if that's not possible, what it could mean if it's not a topical promotion is that you not only did I give you this year's control, I probably gave you next year's control too. You know, so even if you don't want to bicycle them now, you've got two controls waiting in the pipeline that you can retest later when this one begins to flag. So that's great. Last thing, you know, before we sign off is that we've talked a lot about the process from the writer's perspective. But, you know, a lot of our listeners are people that are not directly writing the copy, but they're going to be either cheating or overseeing or hiring writers. And so I'd like to just talk a little bit because you've been on both sides of it and how people in that position can give writers everything that they need in order, you know, to succeed and to create breakthrough promotions. Well, my advice is, yeah, to business owners, marketing people, first of all, don't hire a writer you don't trust. And then secondly, after you've hired a writer that you trust, trust him with every piece of marketing data that could possibly be helpful to him. Your complaint letters, your testimonials, actual response rates and average sale, ROI numbers on past promotions, data cards on the list that you're mailing, including your best list. If you can't trust the copywriter you're working with with this information, then you shouldn't hire him in the first place. If you can trust him with that, then give it to him. Because, you know, those are the things that trigger ideas. Those are the things that give you, that give the writer insight into the prospect. And they also give the writer insight into what's worked in the past, what hasn't worked. You know, another one of the dumbest things that people say is that, oh, we've tested that before. You know, you come up with an idea and you say, why don't we go with a special offer? I won't cash your check for 30 days. Oh, we tested that. Didn't work. Well, let me see the promotion. And then you look at the promotion. They didn't test it. They sent out a half-hearted, crappy piece of copy where there's a one-line throwaway buried in a paragraph on page 23 that, oh, by the way, we won't cash your check until for 30 days to make sure you love the product. That's not a test. You know? And so, you know, look at, you know, be willing to show the copywriter what you've done. You know, dig out the samples, do the work. By the way, Tony, this is a wonderful question because sincere marketers, and I'm talking not about business owners because they'll pretty much move to heaven and earth for you because they have such a huge stake in the success of the promotion you're going to do. The middle-level marketing managers, they don't want to work hard. They're making their 50, 60 grand a year. They don't make much more if you're a success or a failure. And, you know, some of them will work their tails off for you, mostly out of the goodness of their heart or because they're a sincere member of the team. But, I mean, think about it. You've got two copywriters. One is asking you for the Sun, Moon, and Stars, and the other one's just kind of sloughing through it. Which one are you going to like if you're a middle-level marketing manager with no real stake in the results of the mailing? Yeah, he doesn't want to do any more work. I understand. Yeah. So, you know, ask for the stuff. And if you're not getting it, find a way to copy that person's supervisor and say, oh, by the way, I haven't received this yet. When do you think I'll be getting it? You know, but, you know, get the material. But from the company standpoint, you know, go ahead and check the copywriter out. You know, ask around because, and, you know, it is absolutely essential to have a level of trust so that you can share information that will be critical to the success of the program. So, you know, who have you written for before? Get a list. You pick up the phone. You call those companies. They, did you work with them? How was the experience? You know, did they ask for proprietary marketing information? Were they protective of that information? Do you have any reason to believe that there were any leaks of that information? No. Okay. You know, confirm that you can trust this person. You may even write into the contract, you know, a non-disclosure clause. You know, it deprives him of income if, for any reason, he discloses your corporate crown jewels to the competition. And then once you've established, and only once you've established that this is someone you can trust, then lavish them with every piece of marketing data that they asked for and even stuff that they didn't ask for, anything that you think could help them. And I think that's the number one thing companies can do to help copywriters. I think that's great. And one of the things I just wanted to add to that in my experience too is, and I think you've been fortunate for the most part to have worked with clients who understand marketing and are passionate about it. But I think it's important to emphasize that the more the client understands marketing and copywriting, not that they need to know exactly how to do it. That's why they're hiring you. But at the same time, the better overall understanding, and even a lot of the specific understanding they have of it, I think, the better the relationship is because then there can be more accurate communication. There's just more of an understanding and more of a respect. I think that it seems, I think, the process works better when the client is not completely saying, you know, you do, I don't know anything about the market, I don't know anything about this, just you handle it all. You know, like when there's that synergy, he understands marketing, he understands marketing, he wants to know the prospects and all about the prospects and all the different facets of what it takes to create a good promotion. He just doesn't have or, you know, even have the desire or time to necessarily build all the technical know how it takes to write a great promotion. I think that that's important too. Yeah, I think as a copywriter, you have to value the fact, you know, I love ragging on clients because, I mean, let's face it, they've been the source of my greatest joy, my greatest pain for the last 36 years. But I love them. And, you know, a lot of these people are very close personal friends of mine. And the Lord knows when they get together, they probably carp about me too. But the bottom line is that as a copywriter, what you can really highly value about your client is not his copy sense. It's because, again, if he knew how to write copy, he'd be doing it. Why would he be paying you, right? But you can value the input from that client, though, that is enormously valuable. It's his sense of product strengths and weaknesses, his sense of the market's fears, frustrations and desires, and his sense of maybe over, I'd even go so far as to say overall tone, you know, because he knows what's worked in those areas in the past. He knows how his competitors market and what works for them. And you can get valuable input there. And for me, personally, past that point, the best direct response business owner, the best marketing executive out there, their input on the copy itself is no better, certainly no worse, but no better either than a total stranger on the street that I hand the copy to and ask him for it. In fact, I'm going to get a more honest, more visceral reaction of my copy by giving it to a civilian than I am giving it to somebody who's read all the rules about how to write great copy, but can't really do it himself. Sure. So, so. Okay, well, we've covered a lot of ground here, and I think we've given everyone some great information. And I think that this really, you know, helps me. I've even learned a lot on the on the call today, but I think especially in terms of starting projects off well, and really, really making sure, you know, that that projects are initiated with excitement, with momentum, with enough clarity and understanding to produce, you know, really high level results. So thanks, Clayton. I appreciate it. All right, my pleasure, Tony. Take care.
